summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on two very different books that share a common thread: unbridled love for computers. He discusses Russ Walter's 'Secret Guide to Computers,' a rambling, zine-like self-published guide that compares computers to drugs and romantic partners, and Charles Petzold's 'Code,' an elegantly written technical history. Despite their opposite styles—one chaotic and personal, the other austere and beautiful—both authors demonstrate deep dedication to computing. Atwood celebrates this passion, suggesting that genuine enthusiasm for computers, even when it borders on obsession, is something to embrace rather than apologize for.
key_points: Russ Walter's 'Secret Guide to Computers' is a self-published, unconventional guide that has evolved from a 17-page pamphlet in 1972 to a 607-page book, representing decades of genuine enthusiasm
Walter's D.I.Y. ethos and personal touch—including publishing his phone number for 24/7 support and using a 'copywrong' license—outshines polished commercial alternatives
Charles Petzold's 'Code' represents the opposite approach: elegant, austere, and technically beautiful, yet equally passionate about computing fundamentals
Both extremes of computer writing—the rambling zine and the refined technical history—stem from the same root: genuine love for the machine
Passion for computing, even when it seems excessive or obsessive, is something to celebrate rather than hide
money_quotes: Computers are like drugs: you begin by spending just a little on them but soon get so excited by the experience – and so hooked – that you wind up spending more and more to feed your habit.
His no-nonsense, no-budget D.I.Y. ethos outshines any number of polished, soulless commercial books.
Instead of a long, rambling love letter, Code is a collection of elegantly written sonnets.
Sometimes love makes you do crazy things.
I'm with Petzold and Walter. If loving computers is wrong, I don't want to be right.
Marrying a computer is much groovier than marrying a person: computers are good at 'getting it on' and they never argue.
key_insight: The best technical writing comes not from polish or credentials, but from genuine, unashamed passion for the subject—whether expressed through rambling enthusiasm or elegant precision.
